prompts.title: 3-Second Hook Generator (Hindi/Hinglish)
prompts.prompt: You are a senior YouTube Shorts strategist who has scripted 2,000+ viral Hindi/Hinglish Shorts for Indian solo creators. Generate 15 distinct 3-second opening hooks for a Short on the topic [TOPIC] in [LANGUAGE: Hindi / Hinglish / English / Tamil / Telugu / Marathi / Bengali]. The creator's niche is [NICHE: tech / finance / education / comedy / motivation / food / vlog] and target audience is [AUDIENCE: Tier-1 metro / Tier-2 / Tier-3 / college students / young professionals / homemakers]. For each hook deliver: (1) the spoken line, max 10 words, written exactly as the creator should say it on camera; (2) hook archetype label from this list — Question Hook, Contrarian Hook, Curiosity-Gap Hook, Pattern-Interrupt Hook, Stakes Hook, List-Tease Hook, Confession Hook, Number-Shock Hook, POV Hook, Negative-Promise Hook; (3) the on-screen text overlay (max 6 words, sentence case, no clickbait emoji spam); (4) recommended facial expression and energy level (1-10); (5) the visual the camera should hold during seconds 0-3 (selfie close-up, B-roll, prop, jump-cut, etc.); (6) a 1-line retention prediction explaining why a Tier-2 viewer will not swipe in the first 3 seconds. After the 15 hooks, score them 1-10 on Algorithm Risk (would the line trip rage-bait, medical-advice, or financial-advice flags) and pick the top 3 that balance highest predicted retention with lowest policy risk. End with a 60-word coaching note on which 1 hook to A/B test against the creator's previous best-performing Short and what specific metric (swipe-away rate at 3s, average view duration, or shares) to judge the test by within the first 4 hours of publishing.

prompts.title: 15-Second Skeleton Script (Beat-by-Beat)
prompts.prompt: Act as a YouTube Shorts script doctor working with Indian solo creators in the [NICHE] niche. Build a fully timestamped 15-second Short script on [TOPIC] for [LANGUAGE]. Use this skeleton: 0.0-2.5s Hook, 2.5-4.5s Promise/Stakes, 4.5-11.0s Payoff (3 micro-beats), 11.0-13.5s Twist or Recap, 13.5-15.0s Loop/CTA. For every beat output a 4-column table: Time | Spoken Line (exact words, ≤ 14 syllables per second so the creator can speak naturally) | On-Screen Text Overlay (max 5 words, no all-caps shouting) | B-roll / Camera Move / Prop. Beats should average 1 visual change every 1.2 seconds to maintain the retention curve standard for Indian Shorts in 2026. After the table provide: a 30-word voiceover-versus-on-camera recommendation; a loop-back line that makes the last frame visually rhyme with the first frame so the Short auto-replays seamlessly; one comment-bait question that invites a reply without manufacturing outrage; and an Adsense-safe disclaimer line if the topic touches money, health, or law. Close with a 3-line shot list the creator can film in under 20 minutes on a phone, plus the single highest-impact edit (sound effect, zoom-punch, or text-pop) that lifts retention by 5-8% based on common Indian Shorts patterns.

prompts.title: 30-Day Content Calendar (Finance Niche)
prompts.prompt: You are a content planner for an Indian personal-finance YouTube Shorts creator with [SUBSCRIBER COUNT] subs, posting in [LANGUAGE]. Build a 30-day Shorts calendar (1 Short/day) optimised for Tier-2 + Tier-3 viewers aged 22-35 who earn ₹25k-₹80k/month. For each day output: Day number | Date slot | Topic | Hook line | 1-line angle | Format (talking-head / screen-record / explainer animation / street interview / reaction) | Series tag (e.g. #MutualFundMonday, #TaxTuesday) | Suggested CTA (subscribe / watch long-form / comment specific word / DM keyword) | SEBI/ASCI compliance note. Cover this thematic mix: 8 SIP/mutual-fund basics, 5 income-tax (Section 44ADA, 80C, new vs old regime), 4 credit score & credit cards, 3 stock market mindset (no tips, no recos), 3 insurance, 3 budget hacks for ₹50k salary, 2 real-estate vs equity, 2 festival-tied (Diwali bonus, Akshaya Tritiya gold). Tie at least 6 Shorts to scheduled events in the next 30 days (RBI policy date, GST council meet, Budget anniversary, quarterly results week, festival). For every Short add the mandatory disclaimer template that keeps the channel safe under SEBI investment-advice rules and ASCI 2024 influencer guidelines. End with a weekly publishing cadence (best India-time slots), a batch-shoot plan that films all 30 in 4 sittings, and 3 long-form ideas the Shorts can funnel viewers into.

prompts.title: Retention Curve Diagnostic
prompts.prompt: You are a YouTube Shorts analytics consultant. The creator gives you a retention curve and basic stats for one Short: [TITLE], [LENGTH IN SECONDS], [VIEWS], [AVERAGE VIEW DURATION], [SWIPE-AWAY % AT 3S], [LIKE/VIEW RATIO], [COMMENTS/VIEW RATIO], [SHARES/VIEW RATIO], [LANGUAGE], [NICHE]. Diagnose the curve in this exact structure: (1) Verdict — one of Hook Failure, Mid-drop, Late-drop, Loop Failure, Healthy with Ceiling; (2) Evidence — the 2 numbers from the data that prove the verdict; (3) Root cause — written in plain Hinglish so the creator understands without jargon; (4) Fix list — 5 prioritised changes ranked by expected lift, each with a one-line action the creator can apply to the next Short within 24 hours; (5) Re-shoot vs Re-edit verdict — should the creator delete and re-upload, edit and re-upload as new, or leave it and apply learnings forward (explain why with reference to YouTube's 2026 re-upload signal behaviour); (6) A/B test plan — the single variable to change next time and the success threshold (e.g. AVD must beat current by 12%). Finish with a 60-word note on what this Short tells us about the channel's positioning for the algorithm, and one warning if any metric suggests the channel is being categorised into a low-RPM bucket (clickbait, made-for-kids miscategorisation, or rage-bait suppression).

prompts.title: Comment-Bait Without Rage-Bait
prompts.prompt: Act as a community-engagement strategist for Indian YouTube Shorts creators. For the topic [TOPIC] in the [NICHE] niche and [LANGUAGE], generate 12 comment-prompt lines that drive comment-per-view ratio above 1.5% without using rage-bait, communal triggers, caste/religion/region baiting, gender wars, or political polarisation. Each prompt must follow one of these safe archetypes: Opinion-with-Stakes, Vote-Between-2, Personal-Story-Invite, Fill-in-the-Blank, Local-Pride (city/state, never communal), Wrong-on-Purpose-Correction, Pick-Your-Side (low-stakes preferences only), Unpopular-Take Confession, Tag-a-Friend-Who, Numbers-Game (e.g. 'Drop your monthly grocery bill'), Predict-the-Next-Episode, Roast-Me-Politely. For each: the exact line in the chosen language, the archetype label, the placement (in-video voice line vs pinned comment vs on-screen text at the end), the predicted comment quality (transactional / emotional / debate / community), and the moderation risk (Low / Medium / High) plus the 1-line moderation rule the creator should pre-set in YouTube Studio (held-for-review keywords, blocked words). Add a 'reply playbook' showing 3 reply templates the creator should hit within the first 60 minutes to seed conversation: an agree-and-add, a polite-pushback, and a curiosity-question. End with a warning list of 5 phrases popular in Indian Shorts that look engaging but get the channel demonetised or shadow-throttled in 2026, with a clean replacement for each.

prompts.title: Contrarian Hook Builder
prompts.prompt: You are a hook strategist who specialises in contrarian Shorts that earn 8M+ views without crossing into misinformation. For the niche [NICHE] and topic [TOPIC] in [LANGUAGE], craft 10 contrarian hooks that flip a widely held Indian belief (examples: SIP is always safe, government job is best, MBA guarantees salary, gold beats equity, EMI on iPhone is smart). For each hook output: (1) The popular belief in one line; (2) The counter-claim in ≤ 9 words as the spoken hook; (3) The proof the creator must show within the next 8 seconds (data, calculation, screenshot, lived experience) — be specific about which Indian source is credible (RBI, SEBI, NSE, MoSPI, AMFI, EPFO, Income Tax portal); (4) A 'soften-the-blow' line at the 12-second mark that prevents viewers from feeling attacked; (5) A safe-CTA that invites disagreement in comments without being condescending; (6) Risk score 1-10 for backlash, brigading, or fact-check pile-on, with a mitigation tactic for any score above 6. After the 10 hooks, write a 5-line internal checklist the creator must satisfy before publishing any contrarian Short — covering source credibility, tone, demographic of likely critics, and a screenshot trail to defend against false-claim takedowns. Close with the one belief the creator should NOT contrarian-hook in 2026 because it triggers automatic suppression on Indian YouTube (e.g. medical, vaccine, election outcomes).

prompts.title: 30-Second Skeleton Script (Tutorial Format)
prompts.prompt: Build a 30-second timestamped tutorial Shorts script in [LANGUAGE] for the [NICHE] niche on the how-to topic [TOPIC]. Use this proven Indian Shorts skeleton: 0.0-3.0s Stakes Hook ('what you're about to learn saves you ₹X / Y minutes / Z mistakes'); 3.0-6.0s Promise + 1-line credibility flash; 6.0-24.0s Three numbered steps (6s each, each step = setup line + action shot + payoff line); 24.0-27.0s Common mistake to avoid; 27.0-30.0s Loop CTA that primes the viewer to watch again to catch step details. Output a 4-column table — Time | Spoken Line | On-Screen Overlay | B-roll/Screen-record cue. Spoken lines must respect ~14 syllables/second for Hindi and ~16 for English so the audio doesn't feel rushed. Use numbered text overlays (1, 2, 3) in steps because Indian Tier-2 viewers retain numbered structure 18% better. After the table give: a 'first-frame thumbnail logic' line for the 5% who tap to expand; a 'last-frame loop' description that visually loops to frame 1; the exact royalty-safe BGM mood (no copyrighted Bollywood tracks); a captioning instruction for auto-translate to English so the Short discovers internationally; and a 50-word note on whether to add the creator's face or stay faceless for this specific topic, with reasoning grounded in trust-vs-scale tradeoffs for Indian audiences.

prompts.title: 60-Second Storytime Skeleton
prompts.prompt: Write a 60-second storytime Shorts script for an Indian creator in [LANGUAGE] about [STORY TOPIC — e.g. 'how I lost ₹2L in F&O at age 23' / 'the day I quit my Infosys job' / 'when my mother caught me lying about coaching']. Storytime is the highest-AVD format on Indian Shorts but only when paced correctly. Use this beat map: 0-5s Cold-Open Stake ('I almost lost everything because of one WhatsApp message'); 5-10s Setting + Year + Place ('It was 2022, I was in Pune, working at a TCS contract role'); 10-25s Inciting Event with one specific sensory detail per 5s; 25-40s Escalation with stakes doubling at the 32s mark; 40-50s Climax + on-camera emotion peak; 50-55s Lesson in one line; 55-60s Loop hook ('and that's why I never...'). Output a full timestamped table with Spoken Line | Camera (selfie / cutaway / B-roll / text-screen) | Emotion (1-10) | Sound Design Cue. Build in 2 'pattern-interrupt' moments (zoom punch, text flash, sound drop) at the 22s and 38s marks where Indian Shorts typically lose 30% of viewers. Add a content warning if the story involves money loss, mental health, or family conflict, and write the on-screen disclaimer that keeps the Short within YouTube's 2026 community guidelines. End with 3 follow-up Shorts the creator can build into a series from this single story so one shoot becomes a 4-Short franchise.

prompts.title: Question Hook Library (50 Variants)
prompts.prompt: Generate a library of 50 question-style hook templates for Indian YouTube Shorts in [LANGUAGE], grouped into 5 categories of 10 each: (A) Money Questions ('Aapki salary ka kitna percent...'); (B) Identity Questions ('Tum un logon mein ho jo...'); (C) Counter-intuitive Questions ('Kya tumne kabhi socha ki...'); (D) Test/Challenge Questions ('5 second mein bata sakte ho...'); (E) Confession Questions ('Honestly, kya tum bhi...'). Each entry must be a fill-in-the-blank template with [VARIABLE] placeholders so the creator can spin 100s of Shorts from it, plus a worked example using the niche [NICHE] and a 1-line reason the template works psychologically with Tier-2 Indian audiences. After each template, label whether it works better as voiceover-with-B-roll or face-to-camera, and note one niche where it fails (e.g. confession questions flop in pure-tech tutorials). End with a 'do-not-use' list of 8 question hook patterns that are now overused on Indian Shorts in 2026 and trigger viewer fatigue, plus a 60-word coaching note on rotating hook archetypes across a 30-Short cycle so the channel doesn't feel formulaic to repeat viewers and the algorithm doesn't typecast it into one narrow audience pocket that caps growth.

prompts.title: List-Style Hook for Reels-Style Pacing
prompts.prompt: Write 12 list-style hook openers for Indian Shorts in [LANGUAGE] for niche [NICHE] on topic [TOPIC]. List hooks ('5 things...', '3 mistakes...', '7 apps...') are oversaturated, so each must use one of these freshening tactics: (1) Odd numbers (3, 7, 9) outperform even on Indian Shorts; (2) Negative framing ('5 mistakes' beats '5 tips' by 22% AVD on average); (3) Time-bound list ('3 things to do before 25'); (4) Money-bound list ('4 ways to save ₹10k/month'); (5) Outcome-bound list ('5 habits of people earning 1 crore'); (6) Anti-list ('Don't make this 1 mistake — but everyone makes 5'). For each hook deliver: spoken line ≤ 9 words, on-screen overlay number style (large 1-of-5 indicator, progress bar, or chapter dots), pacing rule (each item gets X seconds), and the 'last item is the best' twist line that earns the rewatch. Add a 30-second timestamped script for the strongest hook in the set, with B-roll and text overlays for each list item. Finish with a 'list-fatigue' warning: how many list-format Shorts the creator can post in a 30-day window before the audience tunes out, and the 2 non-list formats they should rotate in to keep the channel feeling varied to both viewers and the recommendation system.

prompts.title: Evergreen Tech Topic List (90 Ideas)
prompts.prompt: Generate 90 evergreen Indian tech Shorts ideas for a creator in [LANGUAGE] targeting [AUDIENCE — e.g. 'college students learning to code' / 'young professionals upgrading laptops' / 'parents buying first smartphone for kids']. Distribute across these buckets: 15 smartphone hacks (Android-first, Indian price points), 10 laptop buying advice (₹35k / ₹60k / ₹1L brackets), 10 free useful apps (verified safe, no scammy VPNs), 10 productivity workflows (Google Workspace, Notion, free India-friendly tools), 10 AI tool how-tos (ChatGPT, Gemini, Indian LLMs), 10 cybersecurity for non-techies (UPI fraud, OTP scam, sextortion), 10 'is it worth it' product debates, 10 hidden settings (WhatsApp, Gmail, YouTube), 5 future-of-work for Indian freshers. Each idea row: Title (≤ 7 words) | Hook Line | Promise | Format (face / screen-record / animation) | Difficulty to shoot (1-5) | Evergreen-ness (1-5) | Series potential (1-5) | Estimated long-form pull-through value (1-5). At the end, pick the top 10 by combined score and propose a 10-Short batch-shoot plan that films all 10 in a single 4-hour session using one phone, one ring light, and one tripod. Add a list of 5 tech topics to AVOID because they invite copyright strikes (showing pirated app interfaces, leaked specs, jailbreak tutorials) or community-guideline trouble in 2026.

prompts.title: Festival-Tied Content Engine
prompts.prompt: Build a 12-month festival-tied Shorts content plan for an Indian creator in niche [NICHE], language [LANGUAGE]. Cover these India calendar moments: Republic Day, Holi, Akshaya Tritiya, Eid, Rath Yatra, Raksha Bandhan, Janmashtami, Ganesh Chaturthi, Onam, Navratri, Dussehra, Karwa Chauth, Diwali, Bhai Dooj, Christmas, Pongal/Makar Sankranti, plus 4 financial moments — Budget day, end-of-financial-year (March 31), Form-16 season (June), advance-tax dates (June 15, Sept 15, Dec 15, March 15). For each event deliver: (1) Best post date and time (some Shorts perform 7 days BEFORE, some on the day, some 2 days AFTER); (2) 3 angle ideas relevant to the niche; (3) 1 'do not do this' angle that risks religious sentiment violation under IT Rules 2021 amendments; (4) Suggested branded-content tie-in if applicable (e.g. Diwali = D2C electronics brands, Akshaya Tritiya = gold/digital-gold apps); (5) Hashtag stack — 1 evergreen niche tag + 2 trending festival tags + 1 regional language tag. Add a regional sensitivity note for festivals that are big in some states and not others (Onam = Kerala, Pongal = Tamil Nadu, Bihu = Assam) so the creator doesn't tone-deaf-post. End with the single highest-ROI festival for the creator's specific niche based on past Indian Shorts trends, with a 60-word reasoning.

prompts.title: Replyable Comment Series Structure
prompts.prompt: Design a 'replyable' comment-response series for an Indian Shorts creator in niche [NICHE], language [LANGUAGE]. The format: creator picks one viewer comment per day, screen-records it as the cold open, and answers it in 30-45 seconds. Build the system, not just one Short. Deliver: (1) Comment-selection criteria — a 5-point checklist (specificity, relatability, controversy-without-toxicity, evergreen value, follow-up potential); (2) On-screen template — exactly how the comment screenshot should appear in the first 2 seconds (size, position, blur the username yes/no, retain the avatar yes/no — note that ASCI 2024 and DPDP 2023 rules require consent if you're identifying real users); (3) 30-second answer script template with 4 beats (acknowledge, reframe, answer with specifics, loop with a new question that becomes tomorrow's Short); (4) Series naming and branding — title card, recurring sound, recurring catchphrase; (5) Volume plan — how many to post per week, how to source comments when organic comments dry up (planted comments from a secondary account is risky — give the safer alternative); (6) Algorithmic benefit — why this format compounds subscriber-to-view ratio over 30 days. Add a sample arc of 7 consecutive Shorts in this series so the creator sees the daisy-chain. Close with a warning about the 1 type of comment that seems like good content but creates legal exposure when answered publicly (medical, legal, financial advice to a named individual).

prompts.title: Daily Fact Channel Architecture
prompts.prompt: Architect a 'one fact a day' Shorts channel for an Indian creator in [LANGUAGE], category [CATEGORY — e.g. India history, money facts, tech facts, body facts, geography]. Cover the full system: (1) Channel positioning — name pattern, tagline, banner copy, channel-page trailer brief (under 30s); (2) Fact sourcing pipeline — 3 credible Indian and global sources per category, citation method that protects against false-claim strikes; (3) 30-second template script with 5 beats (hook fact in 3s, expansion in 10s, surprise twist in 8s, real-world relevance in 6s, loop CTA in 3s); (4) Visual system — same intro card, same outro card, same font, same 2-color palette so 30 Shorts feel like one show; (5) Posting cadence — daily at the same time vs 5x/week, and which is better for India + global discovery; (6) Voiceover vs face — pros and cons specifically for daily-fact format where face-on-camera burns out the creator; (7) Monetisation path — when faceless daily-fact channels in India typically clear ₹0.05 RPM Shorts vs ₹120 long-form CPM via pull-through; (8) AI-assist guardrails under YouTube's 2026 synthetic content disclosure rule — what must be labelled and what need not be. Provide a sample 14-day fact list for the chosen category so the creator can launch this week. End with the 1 thing that kills daily-fact channels in month 3 and how to prevent it.

prompts.title: Week-in-Review Series (Niche Roundup)
prompts.prompt: Build a 'week-in-review' Shorts series template for an Indian creator in niche [NICHE], language [LANGUAGE]. Each Friday/Saturday the creator publishes a 60-second Short summarising the 5 most important things that happened in [NICHE] that week. Deliver: (1) Curation framework — a Sunday-to-Thursday daily 10-minute scan routine listing the 6 best India-relevant sources to monitor for the niche (regulators, industry publications, official handles), with how to spot stories that will still feel fresh on Saturday; (2) Story-selection rubric scoring each candidate on Impact/Surprise/Audience-fit/Visual-availability; (3) 60-second script template with 5 stories at 10 seconds each plus 5s intro and 5s outro, with B-roll/screenshot guidance for each story; (4) Branding system — recurring intro line, 'your weekly ___ in 60 seconds' tagline, end-card that previews next week; (5) Pull-through to long-form — how the Short becomes a teaser for a 8-12 minute weekly long-form on the same channel, including the exact CTA wording that converts Shorts viewers to long-form; (6) Compliance — fair-use rules for showing screenshots of news headlines, watermarking ethics, and the 1 type of story to skip because it crosses ASCI/IT Rules lines; (7) Failure modes — the 3 reasons week-in-review channels stagnate (hot-take fatigue, source bias, late publishing) and how to design around each. Provide a worked example with 5 sample stories for this week in the chosen niche.

prompts.title: Loop-Ending Mechanic Designer
prompts.prompt: Design a perfect loop ending for a Shorts script on [TOPIC] in niche [NICHE], language [LANGUAGE]. A great loop lifts AVD by 25-40% on Indian Shorts because viewers rewatch to 'catch what they missed'. Output 6 different loop mechanics tailored to the topic: (1) Visual loop — last frame is identical to first frame so playback feels seamless; (2) Question loop — closing line asks the question the opening line answered, in reverse; (3) Cliffhanger loop — final 1-second cuts away mid-action and the opening shows the 'before'; (4) Number loop — opening says '5 things' and closing only got through 4, prompting rewatch; (5) Twist loop — final line reveals a fact that recontextualises the opening hook; (6) Sound loop — closing audio sting matches the opening sting so the ear doesn't notice the cut. For each: the exact opening line, the exact closing line, the visual rule for frame-1 and frame-final, the editing instruction (zero-frame cut, 2-frame crossfade, audio overlap), and a predicted loop-rate uplift range. Pick the strongest loop for the supplied topic and write the full 30-second script around it. Add a 'loop ethics' note: distinguish a loop that delivers genuine value on rewatch from a loop that wastes viewer time — the latter is what YouTube's 2026 'low-quality engagement' classifier suppresses. End with 1 loop that the creator should never use because it's now algorithmically penalised.

prompts.title: Repurpose Short to Instagram Reel
prompts.prompt: Take a YouTube Short and rewrite it for maximum performance as an Instagram Reel for an Indian creator in niche [NICHE]. The original Short: [PASTE OR DESCRIBE THE SHORT — title, length, hook, beats, CTA]. Reels and Shorts feel similar but reward different behaviour. Output a side-by-side adaptation table: Element | YouTube Shorts version | Instagram Reels version | Why it changes. Cover these elements: hook word count (Reels needs even faster, ~2s), on-screen text (Reels rewards bottom-third placement to dodge UI), caption strategy (Shorts caption ≈ SEO; Reels caption ≈ engagement bait + 3-5 hashtags max in 2026), CTA (Shorts pushes to long-form on same channel; Reels pushes to Story / DM keyword / link in bio), audio (Reels rewards trending audio strongly, Shorts cares less), watermark (do NOT cross-post with TikTok/Shorts watermark — gets suppressed), cover frame design (Reels grid aesthetics matter, Shorts feed doesn't show grid). Then deliver the actual rewritten Reels script timestamped for the same length, with the new hook, new CTA, and a recommended trending audio category (since exact tracks change). Add a sharing-window plan: post to Shorts first vs Reels first vs simultaneous, with reasoning based on 2026 algo behaviour. Close with a 50-word note on whether this specific Short is worth repurposing or should be left Shorts-only because the format won't translate.

prompts.title: Long-Form Pull-Through CTA Engine
prompts.prompt: Write 15 long-form pull-through CTAs for an Indian Shorts creator in [LANGUAGE], niche [NICHE], whose AdSense earnings depend on funnelling Shorts viewers to 6-12 minute long-form videos on the same channel (Shorts RPM ≈ ₹0.03-0.10 per view, long-form RPM ₹40-200 per 1000 views in this niche). The CTAs must convert without feeling salesy. Generate them across 5 styles, 3 each: (A) Curiosity-Gap CTAs ('Pura calculation main 8 minute ke video mein dikhaya hai — link description mein'); (B) Stakes CTAs ('Yeh galti karoge to 5 lakh ka loss — full breakdown long video mein'); (C) Series CTAs ('Yeh Part 1 hai — Part 2, 3, 4 channel pe complete playlist'); (D) Personalisation CTAs ('Agar tumhari salary 50k-1L hai to long video tumhare liye banayi hai'); (E) Resource CTAs ('Free Excel sheet long video ke description mein'). For each CTA: the spoken line, the placement (last 3 seconds vs mid-roll at 12s vs pinned comment), the on-screen overlay, and the predicted click-through rate range based on Indian creator benchmarks. Add a meta-rule: Shorts that include a long-form CTA convert ~0.3-1.2% of viewers to long-form clicks; the math of how many Shorts views are needed to justify producing a long-form. Close with the 1 CTA pattern that backfires (vague 'check my channel' loses to specific video reference 4-to-1).

prompts.title: Brand Deal Rate Card Builder
prompts.prompt: Build a brand-deal rate card for an Indian YouTube Shorts creator with these inputs: [SUBSCRIBER COUNT], [AVG SHORTS VIEWS PER VIDEO], [AVG LONG-FORM VIEWS], [NICHE], [LANGUAGE], [LOCATION], [AUDIENCE DEMOGRAPHIC SPLIT], [PAST BRAND DEALS IF ANY]. Output a structured rate card document containing: (1) Headline rate table with 6 deliverables — Dedicated Short (60s), Integrated Short (15s mention inside organic Short), Story-style Vlog Short, Long-form Integration (60-90s mid-roll), Long-form Dedicated, Multi-platform package (Short + Reel + LinkedIn). Give 3 price tiers per deliverable in INR — Conservative / Market / Premium — with a 1-line reasoning anchored to CPM math (cost per 1000 organic views) and Indian 2026 benchmarks; (2) Bundle pricing — typical 3-deliverable and 6-deliverable bundles with the right discount %; (3) Add-ons and their prices — usage rights extension (3 months / 6 months / perpetual), exclusivity within category (30/60/90 days), whitelisting for the brand to run as paid ad, scripting rights, raw footage handover; (4) Payment terms — 50% advance / 50% on publish, 30/60-day NET, GST 18% added on top (creator must be GST-registered above ₹20L turnover), TDS 10% under Section 194R for benefits in kind; (5) The 5 questions to ask a brand BEFORE quoting (campaign goal, geo, exclusivity ask, usage period, content control). End with a 60-word note on how to raise rates 30% within 6 months without losing existing repeat clients.

prompts.title: Brand Pitch Deck Outline (10 Slides)
prompts.prompt: Outline a 10-slide brand-pitch deck for an Indian Shorts creator pitching to [BRAND NAME] in the [INDUSTRY] category for a Shorts-led campaign. Inputs: creator's [NICHE], [SUBSCRIBER COUNT], [AVG SHORTS VIEWS], [AUDIENCE DEMO], [LANGUAGE], [PAST CASE STUDIES IF ANY]. Deliver each slide with title, exact bullet copy (≤ 30 words), the 1 visual that should anchor the slide, and a 1-line speaker note. Slides: (1) Cover — creator name, channel, the brand they're pitching to, campaign one-liner; (2) The Audience — demographic + psychographic + 1 voice-of-audience comment quote; (3) The Cultural Insight — a Tier-2/3 Indian insight relevant to the brand's category, sourced (with a real-feeling stat archetype, not fabricated); (4) The Brand Fit — why this creator's audience overlaps with the brand's ICP; (5) The Big Idea — campaign concept in 1 line + 3 supporting Shorts ideas; (6) Deliverables + Timeline — calendar grid; (7) Distribution Math — projected impressions, engagement, CTR, with the assumption shown so the brand trusts the math; (8) Past Performance — 2 case studies, each with brand, deliverable, results; (9) Investment + Bundles — pricing in INR, GST mention, payment terms; (10) Why Now — a closing slide tying to a current cultural moment or brand goal. Add a 1-page email cover note (under 120 words) and the subject line that earns the open. End with the 3 pitch mistakes Indian creators make that get them ghosted by brand managers.

prompts.title: Scope of Work for ₹50k-₹5L Deal
prompts.prompt: Draft a Scope of Work (SOW) document for a brand-creator collaboration between an Indian Shorts creator and [BRAND NAME] for a campaign valued at ₹[VALUE — between 50,000 and 5,00,000]. The SOW must protect both sides and survive scrutiny by an Indian brand's procurement and legal team. Output the full document with these sections: (1) Parties + GSTINs + PAN; (2) Campaign Objective (1 line); (3) Deliverables Table — for each deliverable: format, length, language, platform, organic-vs-boosted, draft-review rounds (max 2), final-approval timeline (48 working hours), live date, geo of distribution; (4) Content Guidelines — brand do's and don'ts, mandatory mentions, mandatory disclaimers (ASCI 2024 #ad / #sponsored / #partnership in the first line of caption AND on-screen overlay); (5) Usage Rights — organic usage only vs paid amplification (whitelisting for X days), territory, exclusivity within category for X days post-publish; (6) Payment Schedule — 50% on signing (advance invoice), 50% within 15 working days of go-live, GST 18% extra, TDS 10% under 194R if barter/freebies are part of the deal, late-payment interest 1.5%/month; (7) Performance Reporting — when and what metrics shared (views, AVD, ER, link clicks, demographic split); (8) Force Majeure + Cancellation — kill fee 25-50% if cancelled after script approval; (9) Indemnity — creator indemnifies brand against creator-caused issues, brand indemnifies creator against false product claims; (10) Governing Law — jurisdiction city, arbitration clause. End with a 1-line plain-English summary the creator can paste in WhatsApp before sending the formal SOW.

prompts.title: Brand Contract Redline Cheatsheet
prompts.prompt: Act as a creator-side contract advisor for an Indian Shorts creator reviewing a brand collab contract worth ₹[DEAL VALUE]. The brand has sent a contract draft. Without rewriting the whole agreement, produce a redline cheatsheet listing the 15 most common predatory or risky clauses in Indian brand-creator contracts in 2026 and exactly how to redline each. For each clause output: (1) Clause name and a typical wording snippet; (2) Why it's risky to the creator; (3) The exact replacement wording in clean legal English; (4) The negotiation script (1 line in Hinglish) the creator can WhatsApp the brand manager to push the change without sounding combative. Cover at minimum: perpetual usage rights ask, unlimited paid amplification (whitelisting), category exclusivity beyond 90 days, morality clause without two-sided symmetry, IP assignment of the creator's likeness, indemnity that includes brand's product-claim risk, late-payment with no interest, kill-fee absent, content edit rights without creator approval, performance guarantees on views/engagement (NEVER agree), exclusivity across all platforms, NDA that prevents creator from disclosing the work even after publish, unilateral termination, jurisdiction in a city the creator doesn't live in, automatic renewal. End with a 5-line summary of the absolute non-negotiables — clauses the creator must walk away from — versus the give-and-take clauses that are fair to compromise on. Add the 1 thing to ALWAYS get in writing on email even if the formal contract is signed.

prompts.title: ASCI 2024 Disclosure Compliance Audit
prompts.prompt: Audit a single Indian YouTube Short for ASCI 2024 influencer-disclosure compliance. The creator pastes: [SHORT TITLE], [BRAND PARTNER], [TYPE OF MATERIAL CONNECTION — paid / barter / affiliate / employee / family ownership], [CAPTION TEXT], [ON-SCREEN OVERLAYS LIST], [SPOKEN MENTIONS]. Run the audit in this structure: (1) Pass/Fail verdict — does this Short comply with ASCI's 'Guidelines for Influencer Advertising in Digital Media' as updated through 2024-2026? (2) Specific failures listed with the exact ASCI guideline clause referenced; (3) Severity rating — Low / Medium / High / Regulatory Risk; (4) Fix list with copy-paste replacements: the corrected first-line caption disclosure (must be in the first 2 lines, before the 'see more' fold), the corrected on-screen overlay (visible for ≥ 3 seconds, in the same language as the content, not buried in fine print), the corrected spoken disclosure if applicable, the right hashtag from #ad / #sponsored / #partnership / #collab / #ambassador / #affiliate (each has a specific use-case — explain which fits this material connection); (5) Special-category overlays — if the brand is a financial product (must follow SEBI investment-advice rules), gambling/fantasy gaming (strict carve-outs and prohibited categories), alcohol/tobacco (prohibited even with disclosure for tobacco, surrogate-ad rules for alcohol), health/wellness (cannot make therapeutic claims), crypto/VDA (mandatory risk disclaimer); (6) The 30-second corrective action plan if the Short is already live and non-compliant. End with a 5-question pre-publish checklist the creator should run on every branded Short going forward.

prompts.title: Finance Shorts Disclaimer Template
prompts.prompt: Create a complete disclaimer system for an Indian YouTube Shorts creator who publishes finance content (mutual funds, stocks, F&O, crypto/VDA, insurance, loans, credit cards, real estate, gold, fixed income). The system must comply with SEBI's 2024 Research Analyst and Investment Advisor regulations, the ASCI 2024 influencer guidelines, and YouTube's 2026 financial-content monetisation policy. Deliver: (1) Master spoken disclaimer template (≤ 6 seconds) the creator can drop into the closing 5 seconds of any finance Short, in 3 language versions — Hindi, Hinglish, English; (2) Master on-screen overlay disclaimer (≤ 14 words) that stays visible for the last 4 seconds; (3) Master pinned-comment disclaimer (longer-form, full-text, includes the line 'I am not a SEBI-registered investment advisor — this is for educational purposes only — please consult a SEBI RIA before investing'); (4) Category-specific add-ons: an extra clause for crypto/VDA Shorts (high-risk, no investor protection, 30% tax flat, 1% TDS), an extra clause for F&O Shorts (90% retail traders lose money — SEBI study), an extra clause for IPO/upcoming-listing Shorts (no recommendation, no tip), an extra clause for tax-saving Shorts (consult a CA, individual situation differs); (5) The exact wordings to AVOID because they trigger SEBI 'investment advice' interpretation even when the creator thinks they're being neutral ('Buy this', 'Will go up', 'Multibagger', 'My pick', 'Sure-shot'); (6) A 'safe-zone' phrase library — neutral verbs the creator can use instead. End with a 1-page personal compliance log template the creator should maintain to defend against any future regulatory query.

prompts.title: Adsense Violation Avoidance Checklist
prompts.prompt: Build a pre-publish 'Adsense and YouTube Partner Program' violation-avoidance checklist for an Indian Shorts creator in niche [NICHE], language [LANGUAGE]. The checklist must catch issues that cause Shorts demonetisation, channel-wide YPP suspension, or 'limited ads' yellow-icon labels in 2026. Output a 30-point checklist organised in 6 categories: (1) Content guidelines — violence, dangerous acts, harassment, hate speech, misinformation (especially on health, elections, vaccines, scientific topics), shocking or graphic content; (2) Advertiser-friendly content — controversial issues, sensitive events, profanity (esp. Hindi gaalis the auto-classifier now picks up), drugs, regulated goods (alcohol, tobacco, gambling — note India fantasy-sports carve-outs), firearms, adult content/innuendo; (3) Made-for-Kids classification trap — unintentionally being marked MFK kills RPM by 60%+; the 7 signals to remove if the channel is NOT for kids; (4) Reused content / spam — daily-fact and faceless channels are at high risk; the 5 originality signals YouTube's 2026 reused-content classifier looks for; (5) Copyright — Bollywood music samples, news clip reuse, movie scenes, Indian podcast clips, viral X/Reddit screenshots — fair use under Indian Copyright Act sec 52 is narrower than US fair use; safe alternatives; (6) Brand integration — undisclosed paid promotion, prohibited categories (alcohol direct ads, real-money gaming where banned by states). For each item: the rule in 1 line, what to check in the Short before publish, and the 1-tap fix. End with a 'red flag' list of 5 niches in 2026 where Shorts monetisation is structurally being throttled and how to pivot the channel positioning to escape that bucket.

prompts.title: CTR Diagnosis for Shorts Feed
prompts.prompt: Diagnose Shorts feed click-through and swipe behaviour for an Indian creator. Inputs: [LAST 10 SHORTS — for each: title, hook line, swipe-away % at 3s, AVD, total views, like ratio, comment ratio, language, niche]. Shorts don't have CTR like long-form, but the equivalents are 'feed-tap rate' (impressions to view start) and 'swipe-retention at 3s'. Run the diagnosis: (1) Pattern detection — across the 10 Shorts, identify the 3 hook archetypes that performed best and the 3 that underperformed, with quantified deltas; (2) Title hypothesis — although Shorts titles matter less than hooks, identify whether title length, emoji use, ALL CAPS, or specific keywords correlate with feed-tap rate; (3) Visual cover hypothesis — the first frame of a Short acts as the cover in suggested and channel-page browse; identify whether close-up faces, text-on-image, or B-roll covers won; (4) Language test — if the channel mixes Hindi and Hinglish, see whether one language consistently wins on swipe-retention and whether to commit to one or maintain the mix; (5) Day/time correlation — see whether posting time correlates with first-hour velocity; (6) Audience-pocket warning — flag if the data suggests the channel is being shown to 2 different audience pockets (e.g. money-curious vs job-seekers) and whether to sharpen positioning. Output: a 5-point action plan ranked by expected lift, an A/B test design for the next 4 Shorts, and a 50-word note on whether the channel is being fed by Shorts-feed (broad cold audience) or by subscriber-feed (narrow warm audience) and what that implies for content choices.

prompts.title: Channel-Page Funnel Audit
prompts.prompt: Audit the channel-page funnel for an Indian Shorts creator. Inputs: [CHANNEL URL OR DESCRIPTION], [SUBSCRIBER COUNT], [AVG SHORTS VIEWS], [AVG LONG-FORM VIEWS], [NUMBER OF LONG-FORMS], [PRIMARY GOAL — pure Shorts monetisation / long-form RPM / brand deals / lead-gen for a course or product]. Audit these 9 channel-page elements and rate each /10 with a fix: (1) Channel name + handle — is it searchable, memorable, niche-clear; (2) Channel banner — does it answer 'who is this for, what do you get, why now' in under 4 seconds on mobile; (3) Channel trailer — is there one, is it under 60s, does it convert browsers to subscribers; (4) Channel description — first 2 lines (the visible part on mobile) and full description for SEO; (5) Featured Short and Featured Video — are the right ones pinned for the goal; (6) Playlists — are Shorts grouped into series playlists that show on the homepage and increase session time; (7) Long-form pull-through path — when a Shorts viewer clicks the channel, what's the next thing they're funnelled to; (8) Sections layout — order of sections (Shorts shelf, latest videos, popular, playlists) tuned to the goal; (9) Community tab usage — frequency, format, engagement. For each item: the current likely state, the target state, the specific edit to make in YouTube Studio in under 2 minutes. End with the single highest-ROI change (usually the channel trailer or the banner copy) and a 30-day metric the creator should track to validate the change worked. Add 1 'do not do' for channel-page in 2026.

prompts.title: Shorts Monetisation Forecaster
prompts.prompt: Build a 12-month monetisation forecast for an Indian Shorts creator. Inputs: [CURRENT SUBS], [CURRENT MONTHLY SHORTS VIEWS], [CURRENT MONTHLY LONG-FORM VIEWS], [PRIMARY NICHE], [LANGUAGE], [POSTING CADENCE], [BRAND DEALS LAST 12 MONTHS — count and total INR], [AFFILIATE INCOME LAST 12 MONTHS]. Forecast across 4 income streams: (1) Shorts monetisation (post Shorts Fund, RPM-based) — model a realistic India RPM range of ₹0.03-₹0.12 per view by niche, with separate scenarios for finance/tech (higher) vs comedy/entertainment (lower) and project monthly Shorts revenue across 3 growth scenarios (Conservative, Base, Aggressive); (2) Long-form pull-through — assume a 0.5%-1.5% Shorts-to-long-form CTR, model resulting long-form views, multiply by realistic long-form CPM by niche (₹40-₹250), project monthly long-form revenue; (3) Brand deals — model deal frequency (1-6/month), average ticket size by subscriber band, and 12-month total; (4) Affiliate + product/course (if applicable) — assume conversion rates from views to leads to sales. Output a month-by-month INR table with totals, plus a stacked breakdown of which stream contributes what %. Add 3 sensitivity flags (what kills the forecast: AdSense suspension, niche-RPM compression, content burnout, regulatory tightening). Include the tax view: under Section 44ADA presumptive (50% of receipts deemed profit, valid up to ₹75L turnover under post-2024 limits with digital receipts condition), what the post-tax monthly take-home looks like, plus GST 18% liability if turnover crosses ₹20L. End with the 3 levers with the highest ROI on the 12-month total.

prompts.title: Hindi vs English A/B Test Design
prompts.prompt: Design a 4-week A/B test to determine whether an Indian Shorts creator should commit to Hindi, Hinglish, or English for the channel's primary language. Inputs: [NICHE], [CURRENT LANGUAGE MIX], [CURRENT AUDIENCE DEMO], [GROWTH GOAL — pure subs / pure revenue / brand-deal-readiness / international reach]. Output a complete experiment plan: (1) Hypothesis statement — what you expect to find and why, given the niche; (2) Variant design — 8 Shorts in Hindi, 8 in Hinglish, 8 in English (24 total over 4 weeks), with topic-matched pairs so the variable isolated is language not topic; (3) Posting protocol — 2 Shorts/day at the same time, alternating language so day-of-week confounds wash out; (4) Metrics to track — primary metric (AVD), secondary (subs/view, comment-quality, brand-deal inbound interest); (5) Audience signal — track the demographic shift in YouTube Studio's audience tab over 4 weeks since language tends to shift Tier-1 vs Tier-2/3 mix and male/female split; (6) Decision rule — what % gap on AVD over how many Shorts is statistically meaningful enough to commit; (7) Intermediate check — at the 2-week mark, what to do if results are inconclusive; (8) Decision matrix — depending on the goal (revenue vs reach), the same data may point to different choices: Hindi often wins on engagement and brand-deal premium for Tier-2/3 brands, English wins on RPM and tech/SaaS brand deals, Hinglish wins on broad reach but caps revenue ceiling. End with the 1 reason creators should NOT actually run this test (it splits the algorithm's audience-pocket model) and the safer single-variable test instead.

prompts.title: Regional Dub vs Subtitle Decision Framework
prompts.prompt: Help an Indian Shorts creator decide whether to expand the channel into regional languages via (a) dubbing using AI voice cloning, (b) creating native versions on a separate channel per language, (c) adding burned-in subtitles, or (d) using YouTube's auto-dubbing feature. Inputs: [CURRENT LANGUAGE], [NICHE], [CURRENT MONTHLY VIEWS], [TARGET REGIONAL LANGUAGES — Tamil / Telugu / Marathi / Bengali / Kannada / Malayalam / Punjabi / Gujarati]. For each option output: (1) How it works in 2026 (YouTube multi-language audio tracks, auto-dub rollout, third-party tools); (2) Cost per Short in INR (own time + tooling cost); (3) Quality risk — uncanny-valley AI voice in Tamil/Bengali still flags non-native viewers as 'inauthentic' which crashes retention; (4) Algorithm behaviour — multi-track Shorts now route to language-specific audience pockets; separate channels build language-specific subscriber bases; (5) Monetisation impact — RPM differences across regional language audiences (Tamil/Malayalam tend to have higher engagement, lower RPM; Marathi/Bengali medium; English-from-South-India high RPM); (6) Brand-deal impact — regional brands (Joyalukkas, Chennai Silks, Aavin) pay for native-language reach; pan-India brands pay for English/Hindi. Build a decision matrix scoring each option /5 on Cost / Quality / Speed / Authenticity / Monetisation. Recommend the right path for this specific creator with reasoning, and a 90-day rollout plan if expansion is recommended. End with the 1 trap: creators who chase regional expansion before solidifying their primary-language flywheel typically dilute both. Define the 'ready to expand' threshold (suggested: stable 100k+ Shorts views/Short in primary language for 3+ months).

prompts.title: Faceless Niche Channel Idea Generator
prompts.prompt: Generate 12 faceless Shorts channel concepts viable for a solo Indian creator in 2026, each fundable on Shorts AdSense + long-form pull-through + 1 affiliate stream. The creator constraints: [DAILY HOURS AVAILABLE], [BUDGET FOR TOOLS], [STARTING LANGUAGE], [STRENGTHS — research / writing / design / voice / video editing]. For each concept output: (1) Channel name pattern (3 examples); (2) Niche and sub-niche; (3) The single insight that makes this concept work in 2026 when most faceless concepts are saturated (e.g. specific audience underserved, specific format the algorithm currently rewards, specific affiliate category with high commission); (4) Content production pipeline — how 1 person produces 1 Short/day in under 90 minutes (research source, script template, voiceover method, visual asset library, editing software); (5) Monetisation primary + secondary streams with realistic INR numbers at 100k subs / 500k subs / 1M subs; (6) Risk score — reused-content risk, AI-disclosure compliance under YouTube 2026 synthetic content labels, copyright risk; (7) The kill-switch question — what would force the creator to pivot or shut the channel. Cover a mix: India business case studies, Indian history mini-stories, Indian geography facts, money psychology, productivity systems, AI tool walkthroughs, app reviews, recipe shorts (faceless overhead-cam), code tutorials, finance terms in 30s, government scheme explainers, scam alerts. End with a ranked top 3 for this specific creator's constraints and a 30-day launch plan for the #1 pick including the first 21 Short topics.

prompts.title: Daily Quote Channel Architecture
prompts.prompt: Architect a 'one quote a day' Shorts channel that doesn't fall into the saturated motivation-quote trap. The creator's positioning: [ANGLE — e.g. Indian-business-founder quotes / Sanskrit shloka with translation / Chanakya Niti / regional poet / contemporary Indian author / specific theme like money/discipline/leadership]. Output the full system: (1) Niche sharpening — why a generic 'motivation quotes' channel will fail in 2026 and how the chosen angle solves the differentiation problem; (2) Sourcing playbook — copyright-safe sources for quotes (public domain, attributed authors with fair-use snippets, original paraphrasing rule), plus a citation format that protects against false-claim strikes; (3) 25-second template — 0-3s dramatic visual + bold text appearance, 3-12s the quote read with paced text reveal, 12-20s 1-line context (who said it, when, why it matters), 20-25s a thought-prompt CTA that earns comments; (4) Visual system — 3 scene archetypes (cinematic stock B-roll matched to mood, animated typography only, illustrated portrait), with consistent typography pair and 2-color palette so 100 Shorts feel like one show; (5) Voiceover decision — own voice in chosen language vs licensed AI voice (with 2026 disclosure label) vs no voice (text + music only); (6) Music strategy — royalty-safe instrumental beds matched per mood (no copyrighted Bollywood); (7) Posting cadence — daily at the same micro-window (e.g. 7:08 am IST for the morning-coffee scroll); (8) Monetisation — affiliate to a curated book list, paid app for daily quote notification, weekly long-form podcast-style breakdown. Include the first 14-day quote/topic plan ready to shoot. End with the 1 reason daily-quote channels die in month 4 (audience saturation by repetition) and the content-rotation rule that prevents it.

prompts.title: 100-Shorts Challenge Plan
prompts.prompt: Design a 100-Shorts-in-100-days challenge for an Indian solo creator launching or relaunching a channel in niche [NICHE], language [LANGUAGE], with [STARTING SUBS] subs. The challenge is meant to find product-market-fit with the algorithm and audience inside 100 days. Deliver: (1) Pre-launch week — the 7-day setup checklist (channel branding, banner, trailer, first 3 Shorts batch-shot, gear, recurring filming slot, batch-edit Saturday workflow); (2) Phases — Phase 1 (Day 1-30) Volume + Variation: 30 Shorts across 6 hook archetypes and 5 formats to let the algorithm learn the channel; Phase 2 (Day 31-60) Doubling Down: identify the top 3 archetype/format combos and concentrate 70% of output there while still testing 30%; Phase 3 (Day 61-100) Series + Compound: launch 2 named recurring series, optimise loops, add long-form pull-through; (3) Daily/weekly rituals — Sunday batch-plan, Monday-Tuesday batch-shoot, Wednesday-Saturday daily edit and publish, daily 15-minute analytics review with a specific 4-metric scorecard; (4) Quitting rules and pivoting rules — the specific signals that justify pivoting niche or quitting the challenge before day 100; (5) Burnout prevention — workload calibrated to ≤ 90 minutes/day on average across the 100 days, and the 2 'rest formats' that keep the channel posting on creatively-empty days (re-edit + repost old hits, comment-response Short); (6) Success metrics — what would make this challenge a clear win at day 100 (e.g. 1 Short crossing 1M views, average AVD up X%, sub-velocity above Y/day, first brand inbound). End with the 1 thing creators get wrong about the 100-Shorts challenge that turns it into 100 days of effort with no learning.

prompts.title: Comedy Short Beat Sheet (15s)
prompts.prompt: Write a 15-second comedy Short beat sheet for an Indian creator in [LANGUAGE] using the comedic premise [PREMISE — e.g. 'Indian dad explaining UPI', 'when the boss says urgent', 'PG roommate vs flat owner', 'startup vs MNC interview']. Comedy on Shorts demands tighter pacing than any other niche. Use this structure: 0-2s Setup (one-line establishing the world); 2-4s Reversal (the unexpected angle); 4-9s Escalation (3 increasingly absurd beats, ~1.6s each); 9-12s Peak Punchline; 12-14s Topper (one extra joke that lands above the punchline); 14-15s Loop tag. Output a beat-by-beat table: Time | Spoken Line (with the joke's exact wording — comedy dies with 1 wrong word) | Performance Direction (face, voice, timing) | Visual/Cut/SFX. Add a separate 'comedy mechanics' breakdown explaining which device powers each laugh: Misdirection / Recognition / Exaggeration / Code-switching (Hindi-English flip is gold for Indian audiences) / Specificity / Callback / Subverted expectation. Provide 3 alternate punchlines for the peak beat, ranked by safety: punchline A = broadest safe humour, B = mildly edgy, C = niche-specific in-joke. Add a 'won't land in Tier-2/3' warning if the premise leans on Tier-1 metro inside-jokes (Bandra, BKC, OYO references) and provide a Tier-2-friendly substitution. End with a 30-word note on character continuity: should the creator build a recurring character for this comedy lane and what name + costume tag makes the recurring bit instantly recognisable in the feed.

prompts.title: Education Short Pacing Map (60s)
prompts.prompt: Build a 60-second education Short pacing map for an Indian creator teaching [TOPIC] in subject [SUBJECT — math / physics / chemistry / biology / history / civics / GK / spoken English / coding / GST / Excel / spoken English / SSC reasoning] in [LANGUAGE]. Education Shorts have higher AVD ceilings than entertainment but only when paced for 'aha-per-10-seconds'. Skeleton: 0-3s Hook ('agar yeh trick aati to 5 marks bachate'); 3-8s Define the problem with a real-world Indian example; 8-15s Show the wrong way most students try (relatability); 15-45s Teach the right method in 3 visual steps (10s each, each step has a visual chunking — handwriting on tablet, animated diagram, real-object demo, screen-record); 45-55s Worked example with a number students will recognise; 55-60s Loop CTA tied to a follow-up Short or long-form full lesson. Output a 4-column timestamped table: Time | Spoken Line | Visual (specific — 'red marker underlines the formula', 'arrow points from x to y') | Pedagogical Purpose (hook / activate prior knowledge / introduce / model / practice / loop). Add a 'cognitive load' check: each beat should introduce ≤ 1 new concept; flag any beat that violates this. Include the on-screen formula/handwriting style guide (large enough for 5.5-inch phone screens at arm's length, high contrast). End with: (1) the long-form companion video outline this Short funnels into; (2) a 'misconception trap' Short idea — the same topic re-shot exposing the most common student error, which historically gets 2-3x the views of the straight tutorial; (3) the 1 phrase to never use in education Shorts because it triggers viewers to drop ('aaj hum padhenge').

prompts.title: Food Short Recipe Format (30s)
prompts.prompt: Write a 30-second recipe Short for [DISH NAME] for an Indian food creator, language [LANGUAGE], cooking style [STYLE — Punjabi / South Indian / Bengali / Maharashtrian / Mughlai / street-food / fusion / healthy / 1-pan / college-budget]. Recipe Shorts have the highest save-rate of any niche (saves are a strong algorithm signal). Beat map: 0-3s Hero shot of finished dish + verbal hook ('Restaurant jaisi [dish] ghar pe — 30 second mein recipe'); 3-6s Ingredient list as bold on-screen text with metric quantities, served visually as a flat-lay; 6-26s Step-by-step cook in 4-5 cuts (no wasted motion, jump-cuts only, hands and pan in frame, audio = sizzle + close-mic ASMR not voiceover-only); 26-29s Final plating + first-bite shot; 29-30s 'Save this' CTA + loop to hero shot. Output a 4-column table: Time | Action on Screen | Voiceover or Text Overlay | Sound/ASMR cue. Include: (1) the ingredient list optimised for India (no '2 tbsp truffle oil' nonsense — substitute desi-pantry friendly), with both English and Hindi names; (2) prep cheat — what to prep BEFORE rolling so the 30-second cook fits; (3) the 'save trigger' — at second 8, on-screen text says 'Save this for [festival/occasion]' which lifts saves 30%+; (4) shoot setup — overhead phone clamp, single warm light, white surface; (5) follow-up content — a long-form 5-minute version with technique deep-dive that the Short funnels viewers to. Add the 1 ingredient claim to AVOID (anything 'cures', 'detox', 'weight loss in 7 days') because it triggers FSSAI advertising-rules issues and YouTube health-misinfo flags.

prompts.title: Vlog Short Slice-of-Life Structure
prompts.prompt: Structure a 45-second slice-of-life vlog Short for an Indian creator in [CITY], language [LANGUAGE], lifestyle stage [STAGE — college student / hostel life / first-job in metro / working couple / new parent / small-business owner / homemaker]. Vlog Shorts win on relatability + sensory specificity, not production value. Beat map: 0-4s Cold-Open with a sensory detail (sound of pressure cooker whistle, autorickshaw horn, metro announcement, alarm at 5:47am); 4-8s One-line context ('Saturday subah, Bengaluru, 15-day-old baby, 4 hours sleep'); 8-30s 4-5 micro-moments shot in vertical handheld, each ≤ 5s, each with a specific texture (chai pour, laptop opening, food scroll, sweater on, traffic); 30-40s One small narrative beat with a tiny stake or surprise (boss pings, baby cries, train delay, small win); 40-45s Reflection in 1 line + loop back to opening sensory cue. Output a 4-column table: Time | What the camera sees | Spoken Line or Text Overlay | Mood/Sound. Vlog Shorts should be ~70% B-roll and ~30% direct address — the creator is tour guide not lecturer. Include: (1) shot list filmable in 1 day with 1 phone; (2) the single 'specific over generic' rule — replace 'metro is crowded' with 'Yellow Line, 9:42, 4th coach' to triple memorability; (3) the 'do-not-vlog' list for safety (don't film bank counters, don't film children's faces without consent, don't film inside Metro stations where policy restricts, don't film at airports beyond designated areas); (4) end-card series tag if this becomes a recurring series ('Day 4 of moving to Bombay'). Add the 1 vlog format that's overdone in 2026 and a fresher alternative.

prompts.title: Pattern-Interrupt Editing Cue Sheet
prompts.prompt: Create a pattern-interrupt editing cue sheet for an Indian Shorts creator working in [EDITING SOFTWARE — CapCut / VN / InShot / Premiere / Final Cut]. Pattern interrupts (zoom punches, sound stings, jump cuts, text pops, color flashes) are how Shorts maintain retention through the 30-50% mid-drop zone. Output a library of 14 named interrupts the creator can apply consistently as a recognisable visual signature. For each interrupt: (1) Name (creator can label them in their own preset library); (2) Trigger — when in the script/structure to fire it (e.g. before a numbered list item, at a contrast moment, on a punchline beat, on a number reveal); (3) Technical execution — exact effect or step-by-step in CapCut/Premiere (zoom %, frame count, easing curve, color, sound); (4) Audio component — the SFX category (whoosh / thud / typewriter ding / cash register / glitch / pop) and the royalty-free source; (5) Frequency cap — max times per Short before it feels gimmicky and hurts retention; (6) Niche fit — which of finance / tech / education / comedy / vlog / food this works best in. Then deliver: a 'rhythm rule' — the average gap (in seconds) between interrupts that maintains feeling-tight without feeling exhausting; a 'silence rule' — a deliberate beat of no movement and no music every 12-15 seconds prevents viewer fatigue; the 1 interrupt currently overused on Indian Shorts in 2026 that signals low-effort to the audience and should be retired. Close with a CapCut preset workflow that lets the creator save 2 of these interrupts as templates and apply them with 2 taps to every future Short.

prompts.title: Affiliate Short Script (Amazon/Flipkart)
prompts.prompt: Write a 40-second affiliate-product Short script for an Indian creator promoting [PRODUCT] from [PROGRAM — Amazon Associates India / Flipkart Affiliate / EarnKaro / Cuelinks / a brand's direct affiliate]. Audience: [AUDIENCE]. Language: [LANGUAGE]. The script must convert without violating ASCI affiliate-disclosure or YouTube paid-product-placement rules. Beat map: 0-3s Problem hook (the pain the product solves, not the product itself); 3-8s Relatable scenario; 8-15s Introduce the product as the solution with one specific feature; 15-30s Demonstrate the product in use (3 micro-shots), highlight the 1 'wow' detail; 30-35s Honest caveat (one limitation — this builds trust and protects against ASCI misleading-claim rules); 35-40s Price + affiliate CTA + disclosure. Output the timestamped script with Spoken Line | On-Screen Overlay | Visual | Affiliate Disclosure Element. Include: (1) The exact mandatory disclosure — first line of caption must say 'Affiliate links — I earn a small commission if you buy. Disclosure as per ASCI guidelines.'; on-screen overlay #affiliate visible for ≥ 3 seconds in the same language as the content; (2) The right link placement — pinned comment with shortened affiliate URL (use the program's official shortener, not third-party that violates Amazon TOS); (3) The 'comparable alternatives' line — even mentioning 1 alternative protects the Short from looking like a paid ad and improves trust; (4) The product categories to AVOID promoting on Shorts — supplements with health claims, multi-level marketing, get-rich-quick crypto, betting/fantasy gaming where state-restricted. End with a 'first-week tracking' plan: which metric proves the affiliate Short worked (clicks, not just views) and the threshold below which the creator should not repeat the format.

prompts.title: Course/Cohort Shorts Funnel
prompts.prompt: Design a Shorts-led funnel that converts cold YouTube viewers into paid students for an Indian creator's [COURSE/COHORT NAME] priced at ₹[PRICE]. Niche: [NICHE]. Language: [LANGUAGE]. Output the full funnel as a 4-stage Shorts plan: STAGE 1 AWARENESS — 8 Shorts that build authority on the problem the course solves, with no course mention; for each: hook line, beat outline, the proof signal that earns subscribe-not-sell. STAGE 2 INTEREST — 6 Shorts that introduce a specific framework or piece of the course IP (the '1 of 12 modules' tease), each ending with a soft CTA to a free lead magnet (PDF / WhatsApp community / email list); for each: lead-magnet hook, capture mechanism, expected conversion rate. STAGE 3 DESIRE — 4 Shorts that show case studies of past students or before/after transformations, with ASCI-compliant testimonial disclosure (the testimonial must be genuine, the disclosure of 'student of paid program' must be visible); for each: storytelling structure, social-proof framing. STAGE 4 ACTION — 3 Shorts during the launch window with the offer, deadline, bonus stack, and a clear price + CTA to a sales page; for each: urgency mechanic that's truthful (real cohort cap, real deadline) not fake-scarce. For the full funnel deliver: (1) 30-day publishing calendar mapping the 21 Shorts to dates; (2) The 4 long-form videos that anchor the funnel (Shorts pull to long-form, long-form pulls to lead magnet); (3) GST treatment — courses are taxable services at 18% GST; the registration threshold and the invoice template; (4) The 1 funnel mistake that crashes Indian creator-course conversions (bait-and-switch hooks that overpromise vs the course actually delivering).

prompts.title: Series Bible for a Recurring Show
prompts.prompt: Write a series bible for a recurring named Shorts show on an Indian creator's channel in niche [NICHE], language [LANGUAGE], show concept [CONCEPT — e.g. '60-second salary review', '5-minute startup', 'Kya kharidu Sunday', 'Dadi ka tip', 'AI for desi people']. A series bible turns one-off Shorts into a franchise. Deliver: (1) Show name (3 options ranked); (2) Tagline ≤ 8 words; (3) Premise (2 sentences explaining the show and the audience); (4) Episode structure — fixed beats and timing that every episode follows; (5) Visual identity — title card, recurring intro animation under 1.5s, lower-third graphic, color palette, font pair, overlay grid; (6) Audio identity — recurring intro sting, recurring outro sting, voice catchphrase opener and closer; (7) Episode formula — the 5-step content template every episode plugs into so producing each new episode takes < 60 minutes; (8) Slate of first 12 episode topics with hook lines; (9) Calendar — release day, time, posting cadence (daily / weekly / 3x week); (10) Cross-platform — does the show air same-day on Reels and LinkedIn or stay Shorts-exclusive; (11) Brand-deal slot design — where in the episode a sponsor integration fits without breaking the show feel, with sample integrations for 3 plausible categories of sponsor; (12) Spin-off potential — how this single show can spawn 2-3 sub-formats (long-form, podcast, live AMA) in year 2; (13) Legacy element — the 1 design choice that makes the show feel evergreen 200 episodes in. End with the 1 reason most creator-led recurring shows die at episode 14 (creator boredom + audience over-familiarity) and the design choice that prevents both.

prompts.title: Section 44ADA + GST Setup Guide for Creator
prompts.prompt: Write a complete tax + GST setup playbook for a full-time Indian YouTube Shorts creator earning ₹[ANNUAL GROSS INCOME] from a mix of AdSense + brand deals + affiliate + course/product. The playbook is operational, not a tax return — written for the creator to set up their finances and stay compliant in FY 2026-27. Cover: (1) Income classification — AdSense as foreign-source professional income, brand deals as domestic professional income, course as services, affiliate as commission; the head of income each falls under; (2) Section 44ADA presumptive scheme — eligibility (specified profession including 'profession of art' interpretation for content creation, post-2024 turnover limit ₹75L when ≥ 95% receipts are non-cash/digital), how 50% of receipts are deemed profit, the books-of-accounts relief, the form ITR-4 vs ITR-3 choice, when to opt out and switch to actual P&L; (3) Advance tax — 4 instalment dates (15 Jun, 15 Sep, 15 Dec, 15 Mar) and the percentage due each, with a worked calculation on the supplied income; (4) GST registration — ₹20L turnover threshold for services, the OIDAR carve-outs, the place-of-supply rules for AdSense (export of services — zero-rated with LUT) vs Indian brand deals (18% IGST/CGST+SGST), the LUT filing process for export-of-services to avoid GST on AdSense; (5) TDS — Section 194R 10% TDS on benefits/perks from brand deals (gifted products), Section 194O for affiliate payments via aggregators, foreign-source AdSense and W-8BEN; (6) Deductible expenses — gear, software, AI tools, internet, electricity %, rent %, editor fees, music licenses, travel for content; (7) Recordkeeping — invoice numbering, GST invoice format, monthly bookkeeping discipline, software recommendations; (8) Year-end — Form 26AS reconciliation, AIS reconciliation. End with the 5 mistakes that trigger Income Tax notices for Indian creators in 2026 and the 1 thing that justifies hiring a CA who specialises in creators rather than a generalist.

prompts.title: Trend Surfing Without Cringe
prompts.prompt: Build a 'trend-surfing' decision framework for an Indian Shorts creator in niche [NICHE]. Trends (audios, formats, memes, news moments) drive short bursts of reach but cringe-trend-chasing destroys channel positioning. For a given trending element [TREND — describe: trending audio / format / news event / meme], output the framework as a 7-question filter the creator runs in under 5 minutes: (1) Niche-Fit — can this trend be wedged into the niche without contortion (score /5)? (2) Audience-Fit — does the creator's audience demographic actually consume this trend (Tier-2 35+ doesn't enjoy Gen-Z dance trends)? (3) Authenticity — can the creator perform this trend without looking like a 40-year-old uncle dancing at a wedding (score /5)? (4) Decay window — how many days before the trend dies; if < 3 days and creator's batch-edit cadence is 7 days, skip; (5) Risk profile — is the trend tied to a sensitive event (death, disaster, communal flashpoint, election) where participation looks opportunistic or breaches IT Rules; (6) Differentiation angle — what's the creator's specific take that 100 other people aren't already doing; (7) Cost/benefit — minutes to produce vs realistic view-lift. After scoring, give the verdict in one of 4 buckets: Skip / Ride Late With a Twist / Ride Now Vanilla / Ride Now Hard. For 'Ride Now Hard' verdicts, output a 30-second script tailored to the niche. End with a list of 5 trend-categories the creator should structurally avoid even when scoring high (anything mocking specific identities, anything tied to a tragedy within 14 days, anything requiring a brand the creator hasn't paid for to appear, anything asking viewers to do dangerous acts) and a 60-word note on the 'trend-fatigue' rule (max 1 trend Short per 5 published Shorts so the channel isn't reduced to 'trend account').

prompts.title: Thumbnail-Style Cover Frame for Shorts
prompts.prompt: Design the cover-frame strategy for an Indian Shorts creator. The first frame of a Short acts as a thumbnail in suggested feeds, browse, and channel-page Shorts shelves — it influences whether a viewer expands a Short. Inputs: [NICHE], [LANGUAGE], [CHANNEL VISUAL IDENTITY]. Output: (1) The 'cover frame rules' — 5 design principles specific to Shorts covers in 2026 (high-contrast subject, ≤ 5 words of overlay text, one bold color accent, face/eye-line if face channel, no clutter near the bottom because YouTube UI overlays the title there); (2) 6 cover-frame archetypes with use-case: Close-Face Reaction, Number-Big Cover, Question-Cover (text-only), Object-Hero (food / gadget), Before/After Split, Curiosity-Blur (something obscured); (3) For the supplied niche, recommend the 2 archetypes that fit best with reasoning; (4) A consistency vs variety trade-off rule — every cover identifiably 'this channel' so subscribers recognise it in feed, but enough variety per Short that the algorithm shows it to non-subs too; (5) Shoot direction — what to do at filming time so a great cover frame is captured naturally (hold a beat at the start, have a clean composed wide shot, leave headroom for overlay text); (6) Editing direction — how to set the cover frame in YouTube Studio after upload, and the difference between the cover and the in-feed first frame (for vertical autoplay, the very first frame of the video also acts as a hook — these should match); (7) A/B testing — even though Shorts doesn't expose thumbnail A/B yet, the way to manually test cover-frame impact across 10 Shorts. End with the 1 cover-frame mistake that visibly tanks Indian Shorts performance — a tiny face in the upper portion of frame combined with a ₹100 freebie watermark from a free editing tool.

prompts.title: Re-edit + Re-upload Decision Tool
prompts.prompt: Help an Indian Shorts creator decide whether to re-edit and re-upload an underperforming Short. Inputs: [TITLE], [POST DATE], [VIEWS], [AVD], [SWIPE-AT-3S %], [PROBLEM HYPOTHESIS], [NICHE], [LANGUAGE]. Output a structured decision: (1) Triage verdict using a 3-question gate — Was the original Short < 1000 views in 72 hours (quality reset is unlikely to recover it on the same upload — re-upload may help); Was the hypothesised problem in the first 3 seconds (re-edit will help) or in the topic itself (re-upload won't help, redo from scratch); Has the channel published > 50 Shorts since (the algorithm has fresh data — risk of cannibalising newer content is low); (2) Verdict in one of 4 buckets — Leave As Is / Re-edit and Replace First Frame Only / Delete and Re-upload as New / Discard and Build a New Short on Same Topic; (3) For each verdict, the operational steps and the YouTube 2026 behaviour the creator should expect (re-uploaded duplicate content gets a 'reused content' suppression risk; deleting and re-uploading the same file with minor edits often doesn't trigger this if the content is meaningfully different — define 'meaningfully' as ≥ 25% new footage AND a different opening 3 seconds); (4) The exact re-edit cut-list — what to change at second 0-3, what to change in the middle drop zone, what to change at the loop; (5) A 7-day measurement protocol after re-publish to know if it worked. End with a 1-line warning: re-uploading in bulk is a strong reused-content signal that risks the whole channel; the rule should be 'rare and targeted, never systematic'.

prompts.title: Collaboration Short with Another Creator
prompts.prompt: Plan a 1-Short collaboration between two Indian creators where Creator A has [SUBS A] subs in niche [NICHE A] and Creator B has [SUBS B] subs in niche [NICHE B], languages [LANGUAGES]. Collabs done well lift both channels' sub-velocity for 7-14 days; done badly they fail because the audiences don't overlap. Output: (1) Audience overlap thesis — how to estimate overlap from each creator's audience tab (age, gender, geo, device, top other channels watched), and a go/no-go rule (overlap should be 25-60% — too low = no resonance, too high = no new audience reach); (2) Format options — Split-Screen Reaction, Tag-Team Storytelling, Counter-Take Debate, Skill-Swap (creator A teaches creator B the niche in 30s, B teaches A back in 30s), Combined POV Skit; recommend the right format for the supplied creators; (3) Single 60-second script using the recommended format with both creators' lines, on-screen overlays, B-roll cues, and a clear 'subscribe to both' CTA framed as 'follow our combined journey'; (4) Cross-promotion mechanics — how to upload (does each channel post the same Short, a different cut, or just one channel hosts), pinned comments tagging each other, channel-page community-tab posts; (5) Equity of credit — name order, screen time split (aim for 45-55), thumbnail/cover frame featuring both, description credit; (6) Conflict-of-interest rule — if either creator has a past brand deal in conflict, disclose; (7) Measurement — what success looks like at 72 hours and the rule for whether to do collab #2 with the same creator or move on; (8) Common collab mistakes specific to the Indian creator scene — 'guesting' on someone bigger without a return invite, mismatched production quality, language mismatch confusing the algorithm. End with the 1 type of collab partner to avoid even when DM-asked (parasocial-only audience that won't convert across niches).

prompts.title: Community-Tab Post Companion to a Short
prompts.prompt: Write 5 community-tab post variants designed to amplify a single Indian Shorts creator's recently published Short [SHORT TITLE / TOPIC] in language [LANGUAGE], niche [NICHE]. Community-tab posts boost a Short's first-24-hour velocity (a key signal in Shorts ranking) by sending warm subscribers to the Short. Output the 5 variants across these strategies: (A) Behind-the-Scenes Image post — the on-set photo with a 30-word caption that teases what only the BTS reveals; (B) Poll post — a 2-option poll where one option is the contrarian view in the Short, driving viewers to watch the Short to see who agrees with them; (C) Quote-Card post — the most shareable 1-liner from the Short as a designed text image; (D) Multi-image carousel — 3 images covering 'before / during / after' with the Short link as the call-to-action; (E) Short Clip post — a 5-10 second teaser pulled from the Short with a curiosity-cliff. For each variant: the exact caption text in the chosen language, the image/clip brief, the placement of the Short link (first sentence vs end vs both), the best post time relative to the Short's publish (suggest 90 minutes after publish to ride the algorithmic boost), the call-to-action wording. Add: (1) Subscriber-only vs all-followers visibility setting and which to choose; (2) Frequency cap — max community posts per week before subscribers feel spammed and turn off notifications; (3) The 1 community post format that's seen as low-value in 2026 (random meme repost with no channel tie). End with how to convert community-tab post engagement into Short performance with a 30-minute window of replying to the comments to push the post higher in the subscriber feed.

prompts.title: Crisis-Response Short (Apology / Correction)
prompts.prompt: Write a crisis-response Short script for an Indian creator who needs to address one of these situations: [SITUATION — got a fact wrong in a previous Short / accused of plagiarism / brand-deal backlash / personal controversy / community-guidelines strike / accidental insensitivity to a community]. Language: [LANGUAGE]. Niche: [NICHE]. The script must defuse without making it worse. Output: (1) Diagnosis of the crisis severity — Low (small factual error) / Medium (audience-trust hit) / High (brand-safety flag, regulator interest, community-guidelines strike); (2) Decision: address publicly via Short vs community-tab post vs silent correction vs do-nothing-and-let-it-pass — give the right call for the supplied severity with reasoning; (3) If a Short is the right channel, a 60-second script with these beats — 0-5s Acknowledge directly without dodging ('I made a mistake in [previous Short]'); 5-15s Specific factual correction or context (not generic 'I'm sorry if anyone was hurt'); 15-35s Why it happened, without excuses (5-10 second self-account); 35-50s What you're doing about it (concrete action — taking the Short down, adding a correction overlay, donating fee from a problematic brand deal, taking a break, working with a sensitivity reader); 50-60s Commitment to the audience and a humble close — no 'subscribe' CTA in a crisis Short; (4) On-screen treatment — sober, no sound effects, no zoom punches, no music or only soft underscore; (5) The 6 phrases that make crisis Shorts worse ('I have always been an ally...', 'taken out of context', 'cancel culture', 'haters', 'as a mother/father', 'it was just a joke'); (6) Pinned-comment template that sits below the Short with the longer-form full statement and links to verified sources for the correction; (7) Post-crisis content cadence — the 7 days after crisis Short, what to publish and what NOT to publish (no celebratory content, no brand deals going live, no flexes). End with the 1 thing that turns a recoverable crisis into an unrecoverable one: deleting the original Short or the crisis Short itself when audiences screenshot first.

prompts.title: Annual Channel Audit + Pivot Decision
prompts.prompt: Run an annual audit of an Indian Shorts creator's channel and produce a pivot-or-double-down decision. Inputs: [CHANNEL AGE IN MONTHS], [SUBS], [12-MONTH SUB GROWTH], [12-MONTH AVG SHORTS VIEWS], [12-MONTH AVG LONG-FORM VIEWS], [TOTAL ANNUAL REVENUE INR + SOURCE BREAKDOWN], [CREATOR HOURS/WEEK], [CURRENT NICHE], [LANGUAGE], [LIFE SITUATION — full-time vs side-hustle, family obligations]. Audit across 8 dimensions, score each /10 with evidence: (1) Audience health — sub-velocity trend, retention trend, comment quality, watch-time-per-viewer; (2) Algorithm relationship — % views from Shorts feed vs subscriber feed vs search vs suggested vs external (ideal mix for the channel goal); (3) Niche clarity — would a stranger watching 5 random Shorts know what the channel is about; (4) Monetisation efficiency — INR per creator hour, vs the same hour's opportunity cost in the creator's day job equivalent; (5) Brand-deal pipeline — inbound rate, repeat client rate, average deal size trend; (6) Content sustainability — burnout signal scoring (Sunday dread, declining quality, fewer ideas); (7) Compliance posture — any strikes, any close calls, any regulatory exposure; (8) Personal alignment — does the creator still enjoy this content category. Output the verdict in 1 of 5 buckets: Double Down / Sharpen Niche / Pivot Format / Pivot Niche / Quit and Restart. For the verdict given, output the next-12-months operating plan: 90-day, 180-day, 365-day milestones with leading indicators, the 3 things to stop doing, the 3 things to start doing, the 1 hire (editor / VA / scriptwriter / CA / manager) that would compound the most. End with the 1 hard truth most Indian solo creators avoid in their year-3 audit (the channel is sustainable but the creator's life is not, or vice versa) and the conversation to have with the family about it.

prompts.title: DPDP-Compliant Email/WhatsApp List from Shorts
prompts.prompt: Build a DPDP-2023-compliant email and WhatsApp list-building system for an Indian Shorts creator who wants to own an audience independent of YouTube. Niche: [NICHE]. Lead magnet topic: [LEAD MAGNET — e.g. free PDF / free Excel / free WhatsApp community / free 5-day email course / free webinar]. Inputs: [CURRENT MONTHLY SHORTS VIEWS], [LANGUAGE]. Output: (1) The legal foundation — DPDP Act 2023 'Data Fiduciary' obligations even for individual creators, what notice + consent must look like at signup, the right-to-withdraw mechanism, the data-retention policy, the breach-notification process, and the difference between WhatsApp Business API contact rules vs personal-WhatsApp DMs (the latter is risky and can violate WhatsApp TOS for unsolicited bulk); (2) The funnel — the 30-second 'lead-magnet Short' template that converts viewers to opt-ins (hook → reveal valuable promise → tell them exactly where to go → repeat the URL twice → loop), with the script written out; (3) Capture mechanism options — landing page on a Notion/Carrd/Wix page with a typeform/Tally form vs WhatsApp join-link via Business API vs Telegram channel — pros, cons, and DPDP compliance for each; (4) The double-opt-in email confirmation flow with copy in the chosen language; (5) The first 5 emails to send a new subscriber, with subject lines and 60-word body each, designed to deliver value before any pitch; (6) Frequency cap — emails/week and WhatsApp messages/week that maximises retention without unsubscribes (Indian audiences tolerate higher email frequency than US, lower WhatsApp frequency); (7) Monetisation rule — the 4-to-1 value-to-pitch ratio that protects the list long-term; (8) Privacy and security — where to store the list (avoid spreadsheet on personal Google Drive), basic encryption, who else has access. End with the 1 reason most Indian creator email lists die in month 6 (the creator pivots to WhatsApp, loses access to the only owned-audience asset that survives a YouTube ban) and the dual-channel rule that prevents it.
